votes.reason: The Python code correctly translates the Coq logic, checking for the exact same prime hex digits and counting them accurately.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, counting the exact same prime hex digits and returning the same boolean judgment.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, checking for the exact same prime hex digits and counting them accurately.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. is_prime_hex_digit checks for the same characters ('2', '3', '5', '7', 'B', 'D'), string_to_list converts a string to a list of characters, count_prime_hex_digits counts matching characters, and hex_key_spec checks if result equals the count. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_prime_hex_digit checks the same set of characters ('2','3','5','7','B','D'), string_to_list converts a string to a list of characters, count_prime_hex_digits counts matching characters, and hex_key_spec checks equality of result with that count. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

All concrete inputs will produce the same results.
Python mirrors Coq: it counts occurrences of the same prime hex digits in the string and hex_key_spec checks equality, matching the Coq spec on all inputs.
Python mirrors the Coq logic: same prime hex digits checked, same string-to-list behavior, same count accumulation, and hex_key_spec checks equality to the computed count.
